region_of_origin: North Pacific and North Atlantic Oceans

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw halibut serving is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one medium fillet portion as commonly sold at retail.

nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values (energy 91 kcal, protein 18.56 g, fat 1.33 g, carbohydrate 0.0 g, sugar 0.0 g, sodium 68 mg, dietary fibre 0.0 g, potassium 435 mg, calcium 7 mg, iron 0.16 mg, cholesterol 49 mg) are sourced from the Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for halibut raw (cr_id F03010021, confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary regional source. These values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175136 (halibut, Atlantic and Pacific, raw), an approved secondary global source, which was used to supplement saturated_fat_g (0.19 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.44 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.47 g), trans_fat_g (0.0 g), and all extended micronutrient values not reported by HPB. Both sources are on the approved source list per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175136 and Singapore HPB Foodo cr_id F03010021
gi_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board Singapore
gi_source.source_match_notes: Halibut, raw contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g as confirmed by both Singapore HPB Foodo (cr_id F03010021) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175136). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. Per Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to null (not 0), gi_category is set to not_applicable, and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by definition. No GI value exists or is needed for this food in the University of Sydney GI Database as pure protein and fat foods are excluded from GI classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (68 mg/100 g) and high potassium (435 mg/100 g) providing a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary targets
hypertension.guidance: Raw halibut is an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals due to its low sodium content and high potassium level; prepare by steaming, baking, or grilling without added salt or soy sauce to preserve its naturally low-sodium profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content with high lean protein (18.56 g/100 g) producing no glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Halibut is an ideal protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contains no carbohydrates or sugar and will not raise blood glucose; avoid batter-coating or deep-frying preparations that add refined carbohydrates and significantly increase glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.19 g/100 g), moderate omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA/DHA), high potassium, and lean protein profile supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Halibut is a heart-healthy lean fish with low saturated fat and a meaningful omega-3 content; regular consumption as part of a balanced diet is consistent with AHA recommendations to include fish twice weekly for cardiovascular risk reduction, and should be prepared without added saturated fats or high-sodium sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) and high-quality lean protein with negligible saturated fat supporting reduction of joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Halibut provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis management per EULAR guidelines; baking or steaming is preferred over deep-frying to avoid advanced glycation end-product formation, and rotating with higher-omega-3 fish such as salmon or mackerel is recommended for greater anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Halibut, raw refers to fresh uncooked fillets of halibut, a large flatfish found in the cold waters of the North Pacific and North Atlantic Oceans. The fish is characterised by its dark brown to grey upper skin and white to translucent flesh with a firm, lean texture and a mild, slightly sweet flavour. Raw halibut is typically sold as boneless fillets or steaks and is prepared by baking, grilling, steaming, pan-searing, or poaching. Nutritionally, halibut is an exceptionally lean protein source providing 18.56 g of protein per 100 g with only 1.33 g of total fat and 91 kcal. It contains zero carbohydrates and zero sugar, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. The fat profile is favourable, with low saturated fat (0.19 g/100 g) and a meaningful omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid content (EPA and DHA) supporting cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health. Halibut is a notable source of potassium (435 mg/100 g), selenium (36.5 mcg/100 g), vitamin B12 (1.16 mcg/100 g), niacin (6.02 mg/100 g), vitamin B6 (0.34 mg/100 g), phosphorus (236 mg/100 g), and vitamin D (4.9 mcg/100 g). Its low sodium content (68 mg/100 g) and high potassium level make it particularly suitable for individuals managing hypertension following DASH dietary guidelines. Halibut is widely consumed across North American, European, and East Asian cuisines and is considered a premium white fish in culinary contexts.
common_user_queries: Is raw halibut high in protein?
Is halibut good for heart health?
What nutrients are in raw halibut?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F03010021, confidence 0.95), an approved primary regional source, with cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175136) for halibut Atlantic and Pacific raw, an approved secondary global source. Both approved sources are in close agreement. Extended micronutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy. No estimation or analogy required for core nutritional values.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as halibut is a pure animal protein and fat food containing no carbohydrate by composition; confirmed zero per both Singapore HPB Foodo (cr_id F03010021) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175136). Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as halibut contains no plant-derived fibre. Trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175136 (no industrial trans fats present in raw fish). Vitamin_c_mg reported as 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175136 (negligible in raw halibut). Saturated_fat_g (0.19 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.44 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.47 g), trans_fat_g (0.0 g), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (15.0 mcg), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.06 mg), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.07 mg), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (6.02 mg), vitamin_b6_mg (0.34 mg), vitamin_b12_mcg (1.16 mcg), folate_mcg_dfe (12.0 mcg), vitamin_d_mcg (4.9 mcg), vitamin_e_mg (0.62 mg), phosphorus_mg (236.0 mg), magnesium_mg (28.0 mg), zinc_mg (0.43 mg), selenium_mcg (36.5 mcg), and water_g (78.97 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175136 to supplement HPB values. Halibut is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; no mandatory non-English local-language names are required per G6. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g); glycemic_index set to null (not 0) and gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. The rheumatoid_arthritis entry from the input had an inconsistency (suitable marked false while reason described anti-inflammatory benefits); this has been corrected and moved to additional_conditions as it is not a mandatory Veritas condition. Iteration 3 fixes: (1) nutrient_source.source_match_notes now explicitly names Singapore HPB Foodo as the approved primary source for core macros and USDA SR Legacy as the approved secondary source for supplementary nutrients, satisfying g2_approved_source_compliance; (2) gi_source now references approved sources (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Singapore HPB Foodo) to confirm the zero-carbohydrate basis, satisfying g2 for GI; (3) glycemic_index remains null (not a numeric value) per the critical rule, satisfying q4.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Halibut, raw record passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1-Q8 scorecard. Core macros are sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo (approved primary regional source, cr_id F03010021) with supplementary micronutrients from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary global source, FDC ID 175136). The gi_source now explicitly references approved sources to justify the not-applicable GI determination, satisfying g2. Glycemic index is correctly set to null per the critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. All zero-macro values are justified in notes. Ontology anchors are defensible across FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT. All three mandatory health conditions are present with specific clinical guidance using correct human condition names. The LLM description is comprehensive and clinically relevant. Alias coverage is consistent between the flat array and aliases_by_language. No mandatory non-English local-language names are required for this food category.
